definition: The galaxy we live in. The Milky Way is a spiral galaxy containing more than 200 billion stars. Our sun is located in one of its spiral arms, about 26,000 light-years from the galactic centre.

An enormous collection of stars, gas and dust held together by gravity. There are estimated to be more than a trillion galaxies in the observable universe. Galaxies can be spiral, elliptical or irregular in shape.
The distance light travels in one year – about 9,500 billion kilometres. Light travels at 300,000 km per second. Light-years are used to measure the vast distances in space.

Our solar system consists of the Sun and the eight planets that orbit it, along with moons, asteroids, comets and other celestial bodies. The four inner planets are rocky; the four outer ones are gas giants.

The process that powers stars. In the Sun's core, hydrogen nuclei fuse to form helium nuclei at a temperature of 15 million degrees. Each fusion releases enormous energy in the form of radiation and heat.
A stream of charged particles that constantly flows out from the Sun. Earth's magnetic field deflects most of the particles, but near the poles they can enter the atmosphere and create auroras.

Glowing light displays at the poles caused by charged particles from the solar wind colliding with air molecules in the atmosphere. In the Northern Hemisphere it is called the northern lights (aurora borealis).

The stage a star enters when the hydrogen fuel in its core runs out. The star expands enormously – its surface temperature drops and it becomes reddish, but its total luminosity increases greatly. The Sun will become a red giant in about 5 billion years.

A gigantic stellar explosion that occurs when a massive star (more than about 8 solar masses) dies. In a fraction of a second, as much energy is released as the Sun emits over its entire lifetime. The light can be visible in daylight.

An object with gravity so strong that not even light can escape. Formed when a very massive star collapses after a supernova. The boundary from which nothing can escape is called the event horizon. Black holes cannot be seen directly but reveal themselves through their effect on their surroundings.

The extremely dense remnant that can remain after a supernova. Electrons and protons have been compressed into neutrons. A handful of the material weighs a billion tonnes. A spinning neutron star that emits regular radio pulses is called a pulsar.

A large cloud of gas and dust in space. Nebulae are the 'nurseries' of stars – under the right conditions, gravity begins to gather gas clouds that eventually ignite nuclear fusion and form a new star.

The prevailing theory of the origin of the universe. About 13.8 billion years ago, all matter, energy, space and time came into existence in an extremely hot and dense point that rapidly expanded. The universe continues to expand today.

A planet that orbits a star other than our Sun. Since the 1990s, astronomers have found thousands of exoplanets. Most are detected indirectly – either by a small dip in starlight as the planet passes in front of the star, or by the slight wobble of the star.
